summary: Gruber pushes back against the narrative that Apple Vision Pro is a commercial flop by pointing out that actual sales (~420,000 units) are almost exactly in line with pre-launch expectations of fewer than 500,000 units. He notes that The Information's own reporter predicted these numbers in May 2023, making the same publication's framing of sales as a 'grand disappointment' disingenuous. Gruber argues that Apple launched Vision Pro for long-term strategic reasons, not short-term sales, given its $4,000 price tag, limited software, and physical constraints on manufacturing. He observes that Apple never launched a major advertising campaign, suggesting the company itself wasn't expecting mass-market adoption. The core argument is that media coverage is manufacturing a 'Man Bites Dog' story out of what is essentially 'Dog Bites Man' — expected results being reported as surprising failures.
key_points: Vision Pro's ~420,000 unit sales are almost exactly what The Information's own reporter predicted in May 2023, making the publication's disappointed framing contradictory
Sony's OLED display manufacturing capacity capped Vision Pro production at roughly 450,000 units per year regardless of demand
Apple never ran a major ad campaign for Vision Pro, suggesting internal expectations were modest and the launch was strategically motivated
No one outside Apple actually knows the company's internal sales expectations, yet media coverage presumes sales are below them
The $4,000 price, limited software ecosystem, and hardware constraints made low initial sales entirely predictable
money_quotes: Apple sells about 1.5× more iPhones on an average day than they'll sell Vision Pros in the entire year.
It's almost as though — hear me out — Apple launched Vision Pro in 2023 for long-term strategic reasons, not with short-term sales in mind.
Nobody compares Vision Pro sales to Apple's expectations, because seemingly no one outside the company knows how many Vision Pro units Apple expected or hoped to sell.

If, against expectations, Vision Pro were today the hottest item on gift wishlists for the upcoming holiday season, you'd have to buy them on the secondhand market at a markup, not at retail price from Apple.
A headline like, say, 'Vision Pro Sales Are Exactly in Line With Expectations' is not going to hit people as a big story, but 'Apple Sharply Scales Back Production of Vision Pro' does.

It's disingenuous, to say the least, for an October 2024 report to suggest that Vision Pro sales are surprisingly weak when they're almost exactly in line with uncannily accurate expectations set in a May 2023 report by the exact same reporter at the same publication.
News publications love to cite their own previous reporting when it turned out to be accurate. Who doesn't love to gloat? I sure do.
key_insight: Media coverage frames Vision Pro sales as a surprising failure, but the numbers almost exactly match pre-launch predictions — including those from the same publication now calling them disappointing.
